name: cal
readme:
Short:        Unix-style calendar display for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Replaces:     util/cli/cal-1.0.lha
Version:      1.32

cal — Unix-style calendar display for AmigaOS

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  cal                  Display current month
  cal 2026             Display full year 2026
  cal 3 2026           Display March 2026
  cal -j               Julian day numbers
  cal -y               Full year display

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = success (RETURN_OK)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: Pure computation and output with zero blocking issues; the only non-trivial change was replacing strptime() (used for parsing month names) with a C89 tolower() loop, since strptime() is absent from libnix. This port also first established the amiport/err.h shim that all subsequent ports reuse.
known_limitations: No highlight of the current day. Year range 1-9999 (same as the original source). Current date detection uses clib2's localtime() and depends on Amiga Locale timezone preferences.

name: cut
readme:
Short:        Extract fields/columns from text for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Replaces:     util/cli/cut-1.0.lha
Version:      1.28

cut — Extract fields/columns from text for AmigaOS

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  cut -d: -f1 file.txt         Extract field 1, colon-delimited
  cut -c1-10 file.txt          Extract characters 1-10
  cut -b1-5 file.txt           Extract bytes 1-5
  echo "a:b:c" | cut -d: -f2  Output: b

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = success (RETURN_OK)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: Clean single-file port with no Tier 2 or Tier 3 issues; mblen() and setlocale() had to be replaced with byte-only equivalents since libnix's multibyte support is unreliable, and strsep() and getline() were inlined as libnix omits both.
known_limitations: -c (character) and -n (no-split multibyte) options operate in byte mode only; no multibyte character support. Maximum line position is 2048 (_POSIX2_LINE_MAX).

name: diff
porting_notes: The Hunt-McIlroy core algorithm was pure C and fully portable, but the port required two new shim functions (fnmatch and scandir), a 256KB stack cookie due to hidden AmigaDOS stack depth, and a case-insensitive filename comparison fix — AmigaOS filesystems never distinguish file case but strcmp() was used throughout diffdir.c. A subtle argv pointer-arithmetic bug in set_argstr() also caused a 4GB allocation on vamos before being fixed.
known_limitations: -I pattern (ignore matching lines) is disabled as it requires a bundled regex library. Long option names (--unified, --recursive) are not supported; use short options. File timestamps in unified/context headers depend on Locale timezone settings.
readme:
Short:        File comparison utility for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Todd C. Miller, OpenBSD contributors (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.95

diff — File comparison utility for AmigaOS

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  diff file1 file2              Compare two files
  diff -u file1 file2           Unified diff format
  diff -c file1 file2           Context diff format
  diff -r dir1 dir2             Recursive directory comparison
  diff -i file1 file2           Ignore case differences

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = files identical (RETURN_OK)
  5  = files differ (RETURN_WARN)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.

name: grep
readme:
Short:        OpenBSD grep (pattern search) for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       James Howard / Dag-Erling Smorgrav (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.68

grep — OpenBSD grep (pattern search) for AmigaOS

Original author: James Howard / Dag-Erling Smorgrav
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE (Amiga 1200).

Usage:
  grep [options] pattern [file ...]

  grep hello file.txt          Search for "hello" in file.txt
  grep -i HELLO file.txt       Case-insensitive search
  grep -n pattern file.txt     Show line numbers
  grep -v pattern file.txt     Show non-matching lines
  grep -c pattern file.txt     Count matching lines
  grep -E "foo|bar" file.txt   Extended regex (alternation)
  grep -F "a.b" file.txt       Fixed string (no regex)
  grep -R pattern dir          Recursive directory search
  grep -B2 -A2 pattern file    Show 2 lines before/after match

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = match found (RETURN_OK)
  5  = no match found (RETURN_WARN)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Note: -Z (decompress) is not supported (no zlib on AmigaOS).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: Multi-file port requiring removal of mmap and zlib dependencies, replacement of fts(3) directory traversal with an opendir/readdir walker, and manual emulation of REG_STARTEND for the Tier 2 regex engine. Two critical bugs were found and fixed post-publication: exit codes were always 0 due to missing braces, and recursive search (-R) silently skipped subdirectories because AmigaOS filesystems never populate d_type.
known_limitations: No gzip/zlib support (-Z flag disabled). Recursive search limited to 32 directory levels. POSIX character classes ([:alpha:], [:digit:]) not supported by Tier 2 regex emulation. Word boundary matching (-w) may not work correctly with complex regex patterns.

name: head
readme:
Short:        Print first lines of files (OpenBSD)
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Bill Joy / UC Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.24

head — Print first lines of files

Original author: Bill Joy / UC Berkeley (OpenBSD head.c,v 1.24)
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE (A1200, Kickstart 3.1) -- 21/21 tests passed.

Usage:
  head [file ...]             Print first 10 lines of each file
  head -n 5 file.txt          Print first 5 lines
  head -20 file.txt           Print first 20 lines (legacy syntax)
  head file1.txt file2.txt    Multiple files with ==> headers <==

Features:
  - Default: 10 lines
  - -n count: specify line count
  - Legacy -count syntax supported
  - Multiple files get ==> filename <== headers
  - Reads stdin when no files given
  - AmigaOS wildcard expansion (#? patterns)
  - Proper exit codes: 0=OK, 10=ERROR

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: An easy port — pure stdio, single file, all Tier 1 — but debugging was complicated by a stale examples/head binary shadowing the port during FS-UAE install, producing ACPU_LineF crashes that appeared to be a code bug. The defensive fix of making buf[4096] static and increasing the stack is good practice regardless.
known_limitations: No -c (byte count) flag, matching the OpenBSD v1.24 source which also omits it. AmigaOS shells do not expand wildcards natively; amiport_expand_argv() handles #? patterns.

name: lua
readme:
Short:        Lua lightweight scripting language
Type:         dev/lang
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan@bowring.us
Author:       Roberto Ierusalimschy, PUC-Rio (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      5.4.7

lua - Lua 5.4.7 scripting language for AmigaOS

Original authors: Roberto Ierusalimschy, Luiz Henrique de Figueiredo,
Waldemar Celes (PUC-Rio). MIT License.

Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

First port of Lua 5.4 to any Amiga platform. Previous ports (5.2.1, 2012)
are 14 years old and two major versions behind.

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on FS-UAE (65/65 tests passing). Standalone binary, no ixemul required.

New in Lua 5.4 (vs old Amiga port at 5.2):
  - Native integer type with bitwise operators (&, |, ~, <<, >>)
  - Integer floor-division operator (//)
  - Generational garbage collector
  - To-be-closed variables (<close>)
  - utf8 standard library
  - Warning system (warn())

Usage:
  lua                           Start interactive REPL
  lua script.lua                Run a Lua script
  lua -e "print('hello')"      Evaluate an expression
  lua -v                        Show version

Known limitations:
  - io.popen() not supported (returns Lua error)
  - No dynamic C module loading (pure Lua require() works)
  - 32-bit integers (range: -2147483648 to 2147483647)
  - os.execute() uses AmigaDOS Execute()

Stack: 256KB (set via __stack cookie)
Binary size: ~253KB

PORT.md documents every transformation applied.
Full source: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport/tree/main/ports/lua
porting_notes: Only 5 of 61 source files needed changes. LUA_USE_C89 cascades integer/number type selection from long long to long/double. Custom lua_tmpnam routes os.tmpname() to T: (RAM disk) since libnix tmpnam() generates broken /tmp/ paths. The -fbaserel flag was tried and rejected — it corrupts A4-relative addressing for Lua's extensive global state (crash-patterns #13). LUA_32BITS (float instead of double) also rejected — generates Line F FPU traps on 68000 (crash-patterns #14). Two perf optimizations applied: localeconv() override (AmigaOS always uses C locale) and tolower() replaced with bitwise OR in pattern matching. Stack increased to 256KB for deep recursion and module loading. 65/65 FS-UAE tests passing on Workbench 3.1.
known_limitations: io.popen() not supported (returns a Lua error gracefully). No dynamic C module loading. Integers are 32-bit (long) rather than 64-bit. os.execute() uses AmigaDOS Execute() with different return code semantics. isatty() always returns true.

name: sed
porting_notes: Moderate port requiring Tier 2 regex emulation and manual REG_STARTEND substring handling; a notable complication was a name collision between the TEXT enum member and the AmigaOS NDK TEXT typedef, resolved by renaming to SED_TEXT. The static regex buffer optimization in regexec_e() gave a 2-5x speedup for substitution-heavy scripts.
known_limitations: In-place editing (-i) is not functional because fdopen() is unavailable in libnix; use shell redirection as a workaround. Regex emulation does not support POSIX character classes, locale-dependent collation, or REG_NEWLINE. No Ctrl-C break handling for long-running operations.
readme:
Short:        Stream editor (text transformation) for AmigaOS
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Diomidis Spinellis / University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.47

sed — Stream editor (text transformation) for AmigaOS

Original author: Diomidis Spinellis / University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator and FS-UAE (59/59 tests passing).

Usage:
  sed 's/old/new/' file.txt          Substitute first match per line
  sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt         Substitute all matches
  sed -n '10,20p' file.txt           Print lines 10-20
  sed '/pattern/d' file.txt          Delete matching lines
  echo "hello" | sed 'y/helo/HELO/'  Transliterate characters
  sed -e 's/a/A/' -e 's/b/B/' file   Multiple expressions

Exit codes (Amiga-native):
  0  = success (RETURN_OK)
  10 = error (RETURN_ERROR)

Note: In-place editing (-i) is not supported on AmigaOS (no fdopen).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.

name: tail
description: Display last part of a file
readme:
Short:        Display the last part of a file
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       Edward Sze-Tyan Wang / UC Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.24

tail — Display the last part of a file

Original author: Edward Sze-Tyan Wang / UC Berkeley
Ported from OpenBSD usr.bin/tail v1.24 (2022-12-04).
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  tail [-f | -r] [-b number | -c number | -n number | -number] [file ...]

Examples:
  tail logfile              Show last 10 lines of logfile
  tail -n 20 logfile        Show last 20 lines
  tail -c 100 logfile       Show last 100 bytes
  tail -n +5 logfile        Show from line 5 onward
  tail -r logfile           Show file in reverse line order
  tail -f logfile           Follow file for new content (polls every 1s)
  tail file1 file2          Show last 10 lines of each file with headers

Notes:
  - The -f (follow) mode uses a 1-second polling interval via Delay().
    Press Ctrl-C to stop following.
  - No ixemul.library required (-noixemul build).

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: The main challenge was replacing kqueue/kevent (a BSD kernel event API with no AmigaOS equivalent) with a 1-second Delay()-based polling loop for -f follow mode. All other issues were mechanical Tier 1 shim replacements, including a new recallocarray() shim.
known_limitations: -f follow mode uses 1-second Delay() polling with no detection of file deletion, rename, or truncation. fpurge() is a no-op so the stdio buffer is not discarded on truncation. File offsets are 32-bit (files >2GB not representable).

name: tee
readme:
Short:        duplicate standard input
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.15

tee — duplicate standard input

Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested on AmigaOS 3.1 via FS-UAE (11/11 tests passed).

Usage:
  tee [-ai] [file ...]

  -a   Append to files instead of overwriting them
  -i   Ignore the SIGINT signal (Ctrl-C on Amiga)

Examples:
  Echo hello | tee RAM:output.txt     ; prints "hello" and saves to file
  Echo hello | tee -a RAM:output.txt  ; appends "hello" to existing file
  Echo hello | tee RAM:a.txt RAM:b.txt ; writes to multiple files at once
  Type file.txt | tee T:backup.txt    ; copy file while backing up to T:

OpenBSD tee v1.15 (2023-03-04). BSD 3-Clause license.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: All issues were mechanical Tier 1 shim replacements; the most interesting change was reducing the I/O buffer from 64KB to 8KB for A500 compatibility (with no throughput impact on AmigaDOS), and adding amiport_check_break() to the read loop so Ctrl-C works between read calls.
known_limitations: -i (ignore SIGINT) works between read() calls but cannot interrupt a blocking read. DEFFILEMODE (0666) is passed to open() but Amiga has no Unix permission model. Buffer is 8KB rather than the original 64KB.

name: wc
readme:
Short:        Count lines, words, and characters
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       University of California, Berkeley (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.32

wc — Count lines, words, and characters

Port of OpenBSD wc v1.32 to AmigaOS 3.x.
Original author: University of California, Berkeley
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in FS-UAE with Workbench 3.1.

Usage:
  wc [file ...]           Count lines, words, and bytes
  wc -l file              Count lines only
  wc -w file              Count words only
  wc -c file              Count bytes only
  wc -h file              Human-readable byte counts (e.g., 1.2K)
  wc file1 file2          Count multiple files with total

Notes:
  - The -m flag (multibyte characters) is accepted but equivalent
    to -c on AmigaOS (no multibyte locale support).
  - Exit codes: 0 (success), 10 (error) per AmigaOS convention.
  - Supports Amiga wildcard expansion in filenames.

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: Easy single-file port with all Tier 1 issues; the main subtlety was that MB_CUR_MAX in bebbo-gcc expands to a runtime function call rather than a constant, so the multibyte character path had to be guarded with #ifdef __AMIGA__ to prevent it from being entered on AmigaOS where wchar/locale support is absent.
known_limitations: -m flag (multibyte characters) is accepted but operates identically to -c (no multibyte locale on AmigaOS 3.x). -h uses a simplified fmt_scaled() implementation rather than OpenBSD libutil. fstat() on directories returns st_size=0.

name: yes
readme:
Short:        Repeatedly output a string (OpenBSD)
Type:         util/cli
Architecture: m68k-amigaos >= 3.0
Uploader:     duncan (at) bowring (dot) us
Author:       The Regents of the University of California (ported by Duncan Bowring)
Version:      1.9

yes — Repeatedly output a string (OpenBSD)

Original author: The Regents of the University of California
Ported by Duncan Bowring using amiport (AI-assisted porting toolkit).
Project: https://github.com/bdgscotland/amiport

Cross-compiled with m68k-amigaos-gcc for AmigaOS 3.x (68000+).
Tested in vamos emulator.

Usage:
  yes [string]

  With no arguments, outputs "y" repeatedly.
  With an argument, outputs that argument repeatedly.
  Press Ctrl-C to stop.

Examples:
  yes              ; outputs "y" forever until Ctrl-C
  yes n            ; outputs "n" forever until Ctrl-C
  yes | delete >NIL: RAM:tempfile  ; auto-confirm deletion

Includes full source code (original POSIX and ported Amiga versions)
and PORT.md documenting every transformation applied.
porting_notes: Trivially small port (17 executable lines) but the infinite loop required adding amiport_check_break() for Ctrl-C support — without it, the user would have no way to stop the program since AmigaOS has no OS-level SIGINT delivery.
known_limitations: Outputs only the first argument when multiple arguments are given, matching OpenBSD behavior (GNU yes concatenates all arguments).
